title: Pineapple Bun Bo Lo Bao
description: Bo Lo Bao, or Pineapple Bun, is a beloved staple of Hong Kong bakeries. It features a soft, fluffy milk bread bun crowned with a sweet, crumbly, and slightly crispy golden crust resembling a pineapple, despite containing no actual pineapple. This delightful contrast in textures and flavors makes it a perfect companion to a cup of milk tea.

instructions.title: Prepare the Bun Dough
instructions.text: In a large mixing bowl, combine high-protein flour, 40g granulated sugar, salt, instant dry yeast, and 10g milk powder. Whisk well. Add the large egg and warm water. Mix with a spatula or in a stand mixer with a dough hook until a shaggy dough forms. Knead on low speed for 8-10 minutes until the dough comes together. Add the softened 25g unsalted butter and continue kneading for another 10-15 minutes until the dough is smooth, elastic, and passes the windowpane test (a small piece can be stretched thinly without tearing).

instructions.title: First Proofing
instructions.text: Form the dough into a ball and place it in a lightly oiled bowl. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp cloth. Let it proof in a warm place (ideally 28-30°C) for 60-90 minutes, or until it has doubled in size. This slow fermentation develops flavor and texture.

instructions.title: Prepare the Pineapple Topping (Bo Lo Bao Crust)
instructions.text: While the dough is proofing, prepare the topping. In a separate bowl, cream the 80g softened unsalted butter and 90g granulated sugar until light and fluffy. Beat in the large egg yolk and evaporated milk until well combined. Sift in the all-purpose flour, 10g milk powder, and baking powder. Mix until a soft, pliable dough forms. Do not overmix. Divide the topping dough into 8 equal portions.

instructions.title: Shape the Buns and Apply Topping
instructions.text: Once the bun dough has doubled, gently punch it down to release air. Transfer it to a lightly floured surface and divide it into 8 equal portions (approximately 60-65g each). Roll each portion into a smooth ball. For each topping portion, flatten it into a thin disk (about 10-12 cm in diameter) on a piece of plastic wrap, or by hand. Carefully place a topping disk over each bun, gently pressing and shaping it to cover about two-thirds of the bun's surface, leaving the bottom exposed for expansion.

instructions.title: Second Proofing
instructions.text: Arrange the shaped buns on baking sheets lined with parchment paper, leaving enough space between them for expansion. Cover loosely with plastic wrap or a damp cloth. Let them proof in a warm place for another 30-45 minutes, or until they have visibly plumped up and are light to the touch. Preheat your oven to 190°C (375°F) during the last 15 minutes of proofing.

instructions.title: Egg Wash and Bake
instructions.text: In a small bowl, whisk together the remaining large egg yolk and 1 tablespoon of water for the egg wash. Gently brush the egg wash over the pineapple topping of each bun. Using a sharp knife or razor blade, lightly score a criss-cross pattern onto the topping of each bun to mimic a pineapple's texture. Bake in the preheated oven for 15-20 minutes, or until the topping is golden brown and the buns are cooked through. Transfer to a wire rack to cool slightly.

tips: For a truly authentic experience, serve the Pineapple Buns warm with a thick slice of cold butter inserted into a split bun (known as Bo Lo Yau).
Do not overmix the pineapple topping dough; it should be crumbly yet cohesive to ensure a crisp texture after baking.
Ensure your yeast is active by testing it in warm water with a pinch of sugar before adding to the flour, especially if it's old.
The scoring on the topping not only adds to the pineapple aesthetic but also helps the crust expand evenly without cracking too much.
article:
## History of Pineapple Bun Bo Lo Bao

Bo Lo Bao, literally meaning 'Pineapple Bun,' is a quintessential pastry deeply embedded in the culinary fabric of Hong Kong. Despite its name and the characteristic criss-cross pattern on its crust that resembles a pineapple, the bun contains no actual pineapple fruit. Its origins trace back to the mid-20th century in Hong Kong's bustling bakeries and cha chaan tengs (Hong Kong-style diners). It quickly became a beloved comfort food, often enjoyed for breakfast or afternoon tea. The bun's popularity grew due to its unique textural contrast: a sweet, crumbly, and slightly crispy topping (the 'pineapple' part) atop a soft, fluffy, and slightly sweet bread base. This ingenious combination captivated locals and visitors alike, solidifying its status as an iconic Chinese bakery item.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe achieves the perfect balance inherent to an authentic Bo Lo Bao. The bun dough is enriched with milk powder and egg, yielding a soft, tender crumb that is characteristic of good quality Chinese milk bread. Proper kneading ensures strong gluten development, allowing the bun to rise beautifully and maintain its airy texture. The magic truly happens with the pineapple topping. Creaming butter and sugar creates a delicate, melt-in-your-mouth base, while the addition of baking powder ensures a light crispness. The precise ratio of flour, sugar, and fat in the topping, combined with the quick bake at a relatively high temperature, results in that distinctively crumbly, sweet, and golden crust that makes Bo Lo Bao so irresistible, perfectly contrasting the tender bun beneath.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

Achieving the perfect Bo Lo Bao requires attention to detail. Firstly, ensure your butter for both the dough and the topping is at the correct temperature – softened for easy incorporation but not melted. For the bun dough, a thorough kneading process is crucial; aim for the 'windowpane test' to confirm optimal gluten development. When shaping the topping, aim for a consistent thickness across the bun to ensure even baking and a uniform crust. Don't press the topping too firmly onto the bun, as this can inhibit the bun's rise. Lastly, the second proofing is vital; allow the buns to fully proof until noticeably puffed before baking, but do not overproof, which can lead to a collapsed texture. A sharp knife for scoring the topping will create clean, aesthetic lines without tearing the delicate dough.

## Serving Suggestions

Traditionally, Pineapple Buns are best enjoyed warm, straight from the oven or reheated slightly. The most classic way to serve Bo Lo Bao in Hong Kong is to slice it horizontally and insert a thick slab of cold, unsalted butter, creating what is known as 'Bo Lo Yau' (Pineapple Bun with Butter). The warmth of the bun gently melts the butter, creating a delightful creamy and salty contrast to the sweet crust and soft bread. They pair exquisitely with a cup of strong Hong Kong-style milk tea (nai cha) or a cup of hot coffee, making for an ideal breakfast, afternoon snack, or light dessert. For a simpler enjoyment, they are equally delicious on their own, allowing the distinct textures and flavors to shine.

It features a soft, fluffy milk bread bun crowned with a sweet, crumbly, and slightly crispy golden crust resembling a pineapple, despite containing no actual pineapple. jsonLd.keywords: Chinese, Chinese, authentic, traditional, baking, Chinese, authentic, traditional, baking, Hong Kong, Bo Lo Bao, Pineapple Bun
recipeIngredient: 250g High-protein bread flour (For the bun dough)
4g Instant dry yeast (For the bun dough)
120ml Warm water (Approximately 35-40°C, for the bun dough)
25g Unsalted butter (Softened, for the bun dough)
80g Unsalted butter (Softened, for the pineapple topping)
1 Large egg yolk (For the pineapple topping)

recipeInstructions.name: Prepare the Bun Dough

recipeInstructions.text: Form the dough into a ball and place it in a lightly oiled bowl. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp cloth. Let it proof in a warm place (ideally 28-30°C) for 60-90 minutes, or until it has doubled in size. This slow fermentation develops flavor and texture.
